lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 2: Image Classification with Linear Classifiers

unit.name: Image classification and the semantic gap
unit.description: Introduces image classification as a core computer vision task, explains why raw pixel tensors are hard for machines to interpret, and surveys the main nuisance factors that break naive pixel matching.
unit.summary: The segment defines image classification as assigning one label from a predefined label set to an image [ts=173s][ts=188s]. It emphasizes the semantic gap between human perception and machine input: a color image is treated as a tensor of pixel intensities, e.g. 800 x 600 x 3 with values in [0,255] [ts=232s][ts=246s][ts=273s]. The lecture then catalogues the major sources of variation that make recognition hard from raw pixels alone: viewpoint changes can alter essentially every pixel even when the object is unchanged [ts=321s][ts=333s], illumination changes modify RGB values through lighting and material interactions [ts=383s][ts=399s], and background clutter, occlusion, deformation, intraclass variation, and context all complicate object identity from a purely numerical view [ts=446s][ts=495s][ts=542s][ts=571s][ts=604s]. It closes by motivating modern data-driven vision: large datasets such as ImageNet made it possible to train models that bridge this gap instead of hand-coding rules [ts=645s][ts=660s][ts=692s].

key_points.text: Image classification assigns one label from a predefined set to an input image.
A color image can be represented as a tensor of numbers, e.g. 800 x 600 x 3 with pixel values in [0,255].
Viewpoint variation can change all pixel values even when the object stays the same.
Illumination changes pixel values because RGB depends on scene material and lighting.
Occlusion, deformation, intraclass variation, and context make raw-pixel recognition difficult.

kp_catalog.name: Image classification and recognition challenges
kp_catalog.description: Assigning an image to a label despite semantic gap, viewpoint, illumination, deformation, occlusion, clutter, and intraclass variation. The task of assigning one label from a fixed vocabulary to an input image.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because image classification and recognition challenges formalizes how assigning an image to a label despite semantic gap, viewpoint, illumination, deformation, occlusion, clutter, and intraclass variation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though roles split between gateway and supporting.

kp_catalog.name: Semantic gap and visual nuisance factors
kp_catalog.description: The mismatch between human object understanding and machine access to raw pixels, compounded by viewpoint, illumination, clutter, occlusion, deformation, intraclass variation, and context.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because semantic gap and visual nuisance factors clarifies the mismatch between human object understanding and machine access to raw pixels, compounded by viewpoint, illumination, clutter, occlusion, deformation, intraclass variation, and context, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
